text: Led a full branch reconciliation between the user's local `main` (a typed engine refactor: `spec_lib` dataclasses, a `steplib`/`_runlib` split, an engine-owned dashboard, and `--status` validate integration) and Michael's GitHub `Edits` branch (months of real use: a complete 61-step spec build-out with A13-A19 renumbering, narratives, a pattern library, typed `_schemas.py`, plus new tools `export_spec.py`, `diff_runs.py`, `ingest_case.py`, `test_steps.py` and a heavily rewritten +1029-line `run_pipeline.py`). The governing principle was "his content, your architecture": Michael's specs were taken wholesale (the user's spec edits were early drafts he had since superseded, so nothing was lost), while his engine features were ported onto the user's typed refactor â€” his runner translated from the old dict API to the `spec_lib` dataclasses with `RunManifest` extended for gate/halt/multi-worker fields, his `_runlib` hardening (depth modes, schema checks on write, retry protocol, 16k section clip) redistributed across the steplib/_runlib split via a clean `schema_check` injection seam, and his Narrative dashboard tab moved into `engine/dashboard/pipeline.html`. The merge landed as a single commit `1f669ad` fast-forwarded onto `main`, `origin/main`, and `origin/Edits` so all three now match, with mypy clean across all 11 engine files and the schema regression harness passing. Three non-blocking follow-ups were flagged: a dashboard gap for the new `awaiting_signoff` state, a pre-existing failure-propagation gap in Michael's parallel scheduler, and `/_status` not exposing new manifest fields; also noted that `pipeline/reference` is a git symlink that degrades to a stub on Windows, falling back to the short prohibited-language list.
